test_id_1.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_1.answer: {"headline_summary": "The Provider Manual, effective October 1, 2025, offers more recent and specific guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims compared to the latest relevant Policy Update from December 1, 2024, and should receive more weight by default.", "policy_definition": "The most recent policy update (December 1, 2024) generally advises practitioners to limit NYS Medicaid claim amounts to the actual invoice cost of the drug dosage administered and to refer to eMedNY provider manual web pages for additional program policy and billing guidance [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. Older policy guidance (August 1, 2021) specified that pharmacies could only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members continuing to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]. Other policy updates (April 1, 2023, and January 1, 2024) direct providers to specific eMedNY Provider Manuals for pharmacy claims guidance [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — Jan 01, 2024].", "provider_manual_definition": "For dual eligible pharmacy claims, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — Oct 01, 2025]. All Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025]. Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments must determine if their business situation or business model requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both policy updates and provider manual guidance indicate that detailed instructions for pharmacy claims, including those potentially for dual eligible members, are primarily found within the eMedNY provider manuals [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — Jan 01, 2024]."], "differences": ["The provider manual provides specific, detailed guidance on payment limitations, Medicare enrollment requirements, and claim submission obligations for dual eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the most recent policy updates primarily refer providers to the manual for such details [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024].", "An older policy update (August 2021) explicitly stated that pharmacies could only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members accessing these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], a specific detail regarding immunization services not reiterated in the provided provider manual excerpts."], "caveats": null}

test_id_2.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_2.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of 2025-10-01, offers more comprehensive and current guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, including payment coordination and provider enrollment requirements, compared to the policy documents, whose most relevant specific guidance for dual eligibles is from 2021 and primarily addresses immunization services. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source dated 2024-02-01, pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines and Medicaid Update articles [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01]. For general pharmacy claims, providers are directed to refer to the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy and the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01]. The policy documents state that for immunization services, pharmacies can only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members, and dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, for dual eligible members, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from enrolled providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01]. All Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources implicitly acknowledge the existence of dual eligible members and their interaction with pharmacy services.", "Both sources refer to the 'NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines' for further guidance on pharmacy claims, indicating a shared foundational document for pharmacy policy [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — 2024-01-01]."], "differences": ["**Scope and Detail of Guidance:** The policy documents provide limited direct guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, specifically stating that for immunization services, dual eligibles access through Medicare and pharmacies bill non-dual eligible members for Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. In contrast, the provider manual offers detailed guidance on payment coordination, provider enrollment requirements, and claim submission obligations for Medicare-covered services for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01].", "**Recency:** The provider manual's guidance on dual eligible claims is significantly more recent (Effective Date: 2025-10-01) and provides specific operational details [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. The most direct policy statement on dual eligibles is older (2021-08-01) and specific to immunizations [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].", "**Provider Obligations:** The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01], and cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from these members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not detail these specific obligations for dual eligible claims within the provided excerpts."], "caveats": "The policy documents frequently refer to external manuals (including the Pharmacy Manual, which is the provider manual source) for comprehensive guidance, suggesting that the policy updates serve as announcements or clarifications rather than exhaustive policy definitions for all pharmacy claims. The specific policy guidance on dual eligibles for immunization services (from 2021) might be a carve-out or specific exception, while the provider manual provides broader guidance for general pharmacy claims for dual eligibles."}

An earlier policy from February 1, 2023, states that Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual eligible enrollees, requiring potential third-party reimbursement sources, including Medicare, to be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. For immunization services specifically, an August 1, 2021 policy indicates that dual eligible members access these services through Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for non-dual eligible members for immunizations [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source, effective October 1, 2025, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources imply that Medicare is the primary payer for dual eligible members, with Medicaid acting as a secondary payer or payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources indicate that providers must interact with Medicare for dual eligible claims, either by billing Medicare first or by submitting claims and accepting assignments for Medicare covered services [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources emphasize compliance with federal and state laws and regulations for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly details payment limits and acceptance, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, no additional payment is made if Medicare is greater, and payment must be accepted in full without recovering Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents state Medicaid is the payer of last resort but do not detail these specific payment rules.", "The provider manual explicitly states that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure dual eligible members receive services and that Medicaid enrolled pharmacies are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents refer to billing Medicare first but do not explicitly state a Medicare enrollment requirement for Medicaid pharmacies.", "An older policy document (August 1, 2021) specifically states that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare and pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for non-dual eligible members for immunizations [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. The provided provider manual excerpts do not contain this specific carve-out for immunization services.", "The newest policy document (February 1, 2024) mentions compliance requirements for 340B drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01]. The provided provider manual excerpts do not mention 340B claims."], "caveats": "The policy documents contain a specific carve-out for immunization services for dual eligibles that is not present in the provided provider manual excerpts. test_id_4.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_4.answer: {"headline_summary": "The Provider Manual, effective 2025-10-01, offers the most recent and comprehensive guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, while the policy documents either refer to the manual or provide older, specific instructions for immunization services. The Provider Manual should receive more weight by default due to its later effective date.", "policy_definition": "For immunization services, pharmacies are only able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members, and dual eligible members will continue to access these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. For general pharmacy claims, providers are directed to refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01; New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — 2024-01-01] and other eMedNY Pharmacy Billing Guidelines [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources acknowledge the existence of dual eligible members in the context of pharmacy services.", "Policy documents explicitly direct providers to the 'NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines' for comprehensive guidance on pharmacy claims, indicating the manual as a primary reference [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01; New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — 2024-01-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual, effective 2025-10-01, provides detailed requirements for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including the federal mandate for Medicaid-enrolled pharmacies to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services, payment limitations (not exceeding Medicaid-only patient amount), and the prohibition against recovering Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — 2025-10-01].", "Policy documents, particularly the most recent ones (2024-01-01, 2024-02-01), primarily refer providers to the Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines for general pharmacy claim guidance, rather than providing specific instructions for dual eligible claims within the policy updates themselves.", "The only specific guidance on dual eligibles found in the policy documents is an older instruction (2021-08-01) stating that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for non-dual eligible members for these services [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]."], "caveats": null}

test_id_5.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_5.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of 2025-10-01, provides more recent and detailed guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims compared to the latest policy updates (2024-12-01, which refers to manuals, or 2021-08-01 for specific dual-eligible guidance), and should therefore receive more weight for current guidance.", "policy_definition": "According to the New York State Medicaid Update August 2021, \"Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" and \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members\" for these services [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. More recent policy updates, such as the New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 and December 2024, generally direct providers to refer to eMedNY provider manuals for pharmacy claims and additional program policy and billing guidance [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01]. Similarly, for 340B drug claims, policy refers to the NYRx Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective 2025-10-01, for dual eligible pharmacy claims, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Furthermore, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources acknowledge the primary role of Medicare for certain services for dual eligible members, with policy stating dual eligibles access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], and the provider manual detailing payment and enrollment requirements for Medicare-covered services for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources implicitly or explicitly direct providers to comprehensive guidance, with policy documents frequently referring to provider manuals for detailed billing and policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01], and the provider manual itself providing detailed policy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["**Specificity and Scope:** Policy documents provide limited specific guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, primarily noting that dual eligibles access immunization services through Medicare and that pharmacies bill for non-dual eligible members for these services [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. In contrast, the provider manual offers comprehensive and detailed guidance on payment limitations, acceptance of payment in full, prohibition of recovering Medicare Part B deductibles/coinsurance, and mandatory claim submission and Medicare enrollment for all Medicare-covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — 2025-10-01].", "**Recency and Authority:** The most specific policy guidance on dual eligibles is from August 2021, while the provider manual's guidance is more recent, effective October 2025, and is explicitly referred to by newer policy updates for detailed guidance [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01]."], "caveats": null}

test_id_6.query: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims
test_id_6.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, effective April 1, 2025, and should receive more weight. Both sources define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims by establishing Medicaid as the payer of last resort, requiring other liable third parties, including Medicare, to be billed first.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy document, New York State (NYS) Medicaid is always the \"payer of last resort\" for pharmacy claims, and federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Specifically for dual-eligible enrollees, \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and Medicaid regulations require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. If there is a \"responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. For dual-eligible patients, the manual indicates that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount for a Medicaid-only patient, and if Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Furthermore, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Providers are also responsible for determining if a patient is covered by other insurance and if that carrier covers the service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].", "similarities": ["Both sources establish that Medicaid is the \"payer of last resort\" for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources state that federal regulations require all other available resources or third parties to be billed and exhausted before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["Policy documents explicitly name \"Medicare\" as a third-party reimbursement source that \"must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" for \"Dual Eligible Enrollees\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. The provider manual, while discussing Medicare payments for dual-eligibles, uses the general term \"responsible third party\" for the primary payor in its newest policy guidelines [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].", "The provider manual provides specific details regarding Medicare/Medicaid payment limits, stating that the total payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate and that Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance cannot be recovered from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. These specific payment rules are not detailed in the provided policy document chunks."], "caveats": null}

test_id_7.query: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_7.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims
test_id_7.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents (newest effective April 1, 2025) and provides more specific guidance for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, indicating Medicare as the primary payer. Both sources consistently define NYS Medicaid as the payer of last resort.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy guidance, New York State (NYS) Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill all applicable third parties liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as federal regulations mandate the use of all other available resources first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, including commercial plans or Medicare, before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]. Through coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — November 01, 2021].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual guidance, for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicare is the primary payer. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Furthermore, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Providers are responsible for determining if a patient is covered by other insurance (other than Medicare) and if that carrier covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that NYS Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, requiring other liable third parties (including Medicare) to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025, by implication of Medicare being primary and Medicaid covering remaining responsibility].", "Both sources imply that Medicare is a primary payer that must be billed before Medicaid for dual-eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly details Medicare's primary role for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, stating that if Medicare payment exceeds the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made, and providers cannot seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Policy documents generally state Medicaid is the \"payer of last resort\" and covers patient responsibility after TPL/Medicare, without the same level of specific detail regarding Medicare payment exceeding Medicaid fees [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — November 01, 2021].", "The provider manual specifies that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the amount for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025], a detail not explicitly found in the provided policy excerpts."], "caveats": null}

test_id_9.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more detailed guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, explicitly stating Medicare is primary and Medicaid is secondary, compared to the newest policy document, effective April 1, 2025, which broadly defines Medicaid as the payer of last resort. The provider manual should receive more weight by default as it is the more recent and detailed source for this specific issue.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy document, New York State (NYS) Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, as federal regulations mandate using all other available resources first. `[New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]` For dual-eligible enrollees, specifically, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, meaning all other sources of reimbursement, including Medicare, must be exhausted and billed prior to billing Medicaid. `[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]`", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicare is the primary payer, and Medicaid acts as a secondary payer. The total combined Medicare and Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]` If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional Medicaid payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]` Furthermore, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid recipients. `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]`", "similarities": ["Both sources establish that Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual-eligible members. `[New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]`, `[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]`, `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]`", "Both sources indicate that other insurance, specifically Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid for dual-eligible claims. `[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]`, `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]`"], "differences": ["The provider manual provides specific details on the payment mechanism for dual-eligible claims, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and no additional payment is made if Medicare covers more than the Medicaid fee. It also explicitly prohibits providers from recovering Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from recipients. `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]` Policy documents, while affirming Medicaid as the payer of last resort, do not provide this level of granular detail regarding payment caps or patient liability. `[New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]`, `[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]`", "An older policy document (August 2021) states that pharmacies will \"only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members\" and that \"Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare.\" `[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021]` This specific phrasing about *only* billing for non-dual eligible members is not present in the newer policy or provider manual chunks, which instead focus on Medicaid being the payer of last resort after Medicare, implying Medicaid *can* be billed secondarily for pharmacy claims."], "caveats": "An older policy document from August 2021 states that pharmacies can \"only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members\" and that \"Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare.\" `[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021]` While this suggests Medicare is primary for immunizations and implies Medicaid cannot be billed for dual-eligibles for these services, newer policy (2023, 2025) and provider manual guidance (2025) generally describes Medicaid as the payer of last resort for dual-eligible pharmacy claims after Medicare, implying Medicaid *can* be billed secondarily. This might indicate a specific scope for the 2021 statement (e.g., only for immunization services) or a policy evolution, with the newer, more general guidance on \"payer of last resort\" being more broadly applicable to pharmacy claims."}

test_id_10.query: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_10.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims
test_id_10.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, provides the most recent and specific guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, superseding the April 1, 2025, policy update by explicitly detailing Medicare's role as primary for certain benefits and setting payment limits.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source dated April 1, 2025, New York State (NYS) Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Earlier policy guidance from November 1, 2021, clarifies that through coordination of benefits (COB), Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which includes Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, for dual-eligible patients, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].", "similarities": ["Both sources establish that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill other liable third parties, including Medicare, before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] (implied by Medicare/Medicaid payment rules and non-recovery of Part B costs).", "Both emphasize the provider's responsibility to actively determine and bill other insurance carriers, including Medicare, for services rendered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23]."], "differences": ["The provider manual provides more specific instructions for dual-eligible patients regarding Medicare Part B, explicitly stating that providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid, and that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents generally state Medicaid is the payer of last resort and may cover patient responsibility or excluded drugs, but do not detail the Part B deductible/coinsurance rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01].", "Policy documents explicitly state that Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the TPL Plan, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01]. While the provider manual implies Medicaid's secondary role, its focus for dual-eligibles is more on payment limits and the non-recovery of Medicare Part B costs from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]."], "caveats": null}

test_id_11.query: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?
test_id_11.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more recent and specific guidance for pharmacy providers regarding co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, stating they cannot recover Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance. This should receive more weight than the newest relevant policy guidance from May 1, 2024, which similarly prohibits providers of Medicare Part C benefits from recovering co-payments or co-insurance from dual-eligible individuals.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest relevant policy guidance effective May 1, 2024, a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals, and is required to accept the Medicare payment [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]. Generally, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, meaning all other sources of reimbursement, including Medicare, must be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual guidance effective October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible members, as the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. For Medicare Part D members, Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that providers cannot recover certain cost-sharing amounts (co-payments, co-insurance, deductibles) directly from dual-eligible members for services covered by Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].", "Both imply that the Medicare and Medicaid payments combined are considered payment in full for services rendered to dual-eligible members, with no additional recovery from the member [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy guidance, effective May 1, 2024, specifically addresses providers of a *Medicare Part C benefit*, stating they cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024].", "The provider manual guidance, effective October 1, 2025, specifically addresses *pharmacy providers* of a *Medicare Part B benefit*, stating they cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. It also provides specific rules for Medicare Part D members regarding drug coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025].", "The provider manual guidance is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the most relevant policy guidance (effective May 1, 2024) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]."], "caveats": "The provided documents offer specific guidance for certain types of providers (Medicare Part C providers in policy, pharmacy providers for Medicare Part B/D in manual) and benefit types. Neither source provides a comprehensive, general definition of co-payment responsibility for *all* dual-eligible members across *all* service types."}

Medicaid generally functions as the payer of last resort, meaning it makes payments only after all other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, have been exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, states that \"a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me [...]\" dual-eligible members, and that the \"Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. While providers may attempt to collect outstanding general Medicaid copayments, pharmacy providers \"must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is collected from a Medicaid member\" as the co-payment is automatically deducted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that providers cannot recover certain Medicare co-payment or coinsurance amounts directly from dual-eligible individuals. The policy documents state that a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit \"cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co- insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals\" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01]. test_id_16.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more recent and specific guidance on Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, stating that Medicaid only covers drugs specifically excluded by law from Part D plans. Policy documents, with the newest relevant guidance from July 1, 2020, offer a general rule that Medicaid will not cover claims if the primary insurer does not, but lack specific details on Part D-excluded drugs. test_id_20.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, offers specific guidance on Medicaid's coverage of drugs legally excluded from Medicare Part D, while the most relevant policy documents from July 1, 2020, provide general rules on Medicaid's secondary payer role. The provider manual is more recent and directly addresses the target concept, and should therefore receive more weight for current guidance.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy document, effective February 1, 2025, does not provide specific guidance on Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, focusing instead on copayment overrides [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]. Older policy guidance states that if the primary insurer (Medicare) does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]. Providers are instructed to bill Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs and Part D for Part D covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]. This implies Medicaid is generally secondary and does not cover drugs Medicare *should* cover, but it does not explicitly address drugs *excluded by law* from Part D.", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, it is generally not appropriate to bill Medicaid for prescription drugs for a Medicare Part D member, as most drug costs are paid by Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. However, Medicaid *will* pay for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is \"specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. Examples of such excluded drugs include \"Select prescription vitamins\" and \"Certain non-prescription drugs\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. For dual-eligible members, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the amount for a Medicaid-only patient, and if Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources imply that Medicaid generally acts as a secondary payer when Medicare coverage is available for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly defines and provides examples of \"Medicare Part D-excluded drugs\" that Medicaid *will* cover, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].", "The policy documents, particularly the most relevant ones from July 2020, do not explicitly define or provide specific guidance on Medicaid's coverage for drugs *excluded by law* from Medicare Part D, instead stating a general rule that if Medicare doesn't cover a drug, Medicaid won't either [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]. test_id_21.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare
test_id_21.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an Effective Date of 2025-10-01, is more recent than the newest policy document (Effective Date 2023-02-01) and should guide current practice. Both sources consistently mandate that Medicare must be billed prior to Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy document, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means all other sources of reimbursement, including Medicare, \"must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" for dual eligible enrollees, pursuant to federal law [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. Earlier policy also indicates that for dual eligible members, immunization services are accessed through Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. Pharmacies have a general responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which includes Medicare or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual explicitly states that \"Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid\" and that \"Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. This requirement is analogous to Third Party Liability (TPL), where pharmacies must submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]. The manual further clarifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater, no additional payment will be made, reinforcing Medicare as the primary payer [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both the newest policy (Effective Date 2023-02-01) and provider manual (Effective Date 2025-10-01) documents explicitly state that Medicare must be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources align with the principle that Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, requiring other reimbursement sources to be exhausted first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The policy document specifically frames the requirement for billing Medicare prior to Medicaid in the context of \"dual eligible enrollees\" and cites \"federal law\" for Medicaid being the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].", "The provider manual states the requirement more broadly for \"Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid\" and emphasizes maximizing Medicare benefits, without explicitly limiting the primary statement to dual eligible enrollees [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]."], "caveats": null}

test_id_22.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.

test_id_22.answer: {"headline_summary": "The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual (effective October 1, 2025) is more recent than the newest policy document (effective April 1, 2025) and should receive more weight. Both sources consistently state that pharmacies must bill Medicare before billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source, New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort and federal regulations require all other available resources to be used first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]. Through COB, NYS Medicaid ensures the correct party pays first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]. When Medicaid does pay, it pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — 2022-10-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "For pharmacy claims, if a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. If a drug claim is not related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case (e.g., a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition), NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources establish that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual provides specific instructions for COB related to Workers’ Compensation cases for pharmacy claims, including when Workers' Compensation must be utilized and when NYRx may be billed as primary with a specific Eligibility Clarification Code [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "The policy documents state that NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — 2022-10-01], a detail not present in the provided provider manual excerpts.", "The policy documents provide a general requirement for all NYS Medicaid providers to bill applicable third parties [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01], while the provider manual focuses specifically on pharmacy claims and Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - test_id_32.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents, effective April 1, 2025, and should receive more weight by default. Both sources establish that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill other liable third parties first, with the provider manual adding specific guidance for Workers' Compensation cases in the NYRx Pharmacy Program.", "policy_definition": "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 01, 2025]. Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first, meaning if there is a responsible third-party, such as a health insurance provider, they should pay first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 01, 2022]. The NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, specifically needs to ensure accurate COB submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:5 — May 01, 2023].", "provider_manual_definition": "For members with a work-related injury and an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 01, 2025]. If a drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that NYS Medicaid is always the payer/payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 01, 2022].", "Both sources require providers to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 01, 2022]."], "differences": ["The provider manual provides specific COB requirements for Workers’ Compensation cases within the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program, detailing when Workers' Compensation must be utilized first and when NYRx may be billed as primary [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 01, 2025].", "Policy documents provide a general requirement for COB, stating that all other available resources must be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment and that responsible third-parties like health insurance providers should pay first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — April 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — October 01, 2022]. While policy mentions NYRx pharmacy COB submissions, it does not detail specific scenarios like Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:5 — May 01, 2023]."], "caveats": "The specific guidance in the provider manual regarding Workers' Compensation and primary payer status is explicitly for the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program, while the policy documents outline broader COB principles applicable to NYS Medicaid generally."}

test_id_35.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document dated April 1, 2025, and should receive more weight by default for current guidance. While policy documents provide general requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB) across NYS Medicaid, the provider manual specifically details COB requirements for Workers' Compensation within the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source dated April 1, 2025, NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]. COB ensures the correct party pays first, meaning if a responsible third-party, such as a health insurance provider, should pay for health benefits, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — 2022-10-01]. NYS Medicaid then pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — 2022-10-01]. For Fee-for-Service Dental claims, the COB claim submitted to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB) [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, for members with a work-related injury and an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. If a drug claim is not related to the member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources implicitly or explicitly state that other available coverage or liable third parties should be utilized before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["**Scope and Specificity:** Policy documents provide a broad definition of COB, stating that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requiring providers to bill all applicable third parties before Medicaid, including general health insurance and specific guidance for Fee-for-Service Dental claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — 2022-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01]. In contrast, the provided provider manual context focuses specifically on COB requirements for Workers’ Compensation cases within the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program, detailing when Workers' Compensation must be billed first and when NYRx may be billed as primary for unrelated conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]."], "caveats": "The provided provider manual context is highly specific to Workers' Compensation within the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program and does not offer a general definition of COB requirements applicable across all services, unlike the policy documents."}

test_id_36.query: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing
test_id_36.answer: {"headline_summary": "The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, effective July 1, 2023, and therefore should receive more weight. The Provider Manual provides specific guidance for missing patient demographic information and verifying unknown prescribers, while Policy focuses on notifying prescribers for missing prior authorization for non-preferred drugs.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy document, New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12, effective July 1, 2023, when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization (PA), the pharmacist \"must notify the prescriber\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01]. Other policy documents outline general responsibilities for pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists, such as ensuring \"recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained\" and that the pharmacy is under immediate supervision [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — 2023-04-01], and that the supervising pharmacist is \"responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy\" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — 2023-05-01], but do not specifically detail actions for missing patient or prescriber information on a prescription itself.", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective October 1, 2025, if the address, age, or CIN of a Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist \"is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription\" if the information is \"otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled,\" or if \"The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. Furthermore, the pharmacist \"is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01]. Prescriptions for controlled substances must also meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources imply a general responsibility for pharmacists to ensure the legitimacy and proper processing of prescriptions, whether by notifying prescribers for prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01] or by verifying prescription validity and prescriber identity [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources place a general responsibility on pharmacists (or supervising pharmacists) for maintaining recognized standards of practice and proper conduct of the pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — 2023-04-01; New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — 2023-05-01], which would implicitly cover handling situations with missing information appropriately."], "differences": ["The Provider Manual explicitly details actions for missing patient-specific demographic information (address, age, or CIN), stating pharmacists are not required to enter it if readily available or if the prescription is otherwise deemed legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not provide specific guidance for these types of missing patient details.", "The Provider Manual explicitly requires pharmacists to make a \"good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not contain this specific instruction for unknown prescribers.", "Policy documents specifically address the responsibility to \"notify the prescriber\" when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01], which is a form of missing prescriber-related information (the PA itself). The Provider Manual does not explicitly mention this specific scenario in the provided chunks."], "caveats": null}

test_id_38.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, offers more recent and specific guidance on pharmacist responsibilities for missing patient or prescriber information compared to the policy documents, with the newest relevant policy effective January 1, 2025. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy document, \"New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1\" (effective 2025-01-01), for prescriptions written as part of an emergency department visit where the assigned Primary Care Provider (PCP) is unavailable (e.g., after hours) to verify, the pharmacy is responsible for \"using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements\" and for \"contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient\" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01]. Other policy documents discuss general pharmacist responsibilities, such as notifying the prescriber for non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01], but do not specifically address missing patient or prescriber information on a prescription in the same context as the provider manual.", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, \"New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines\" (effective 2025-10-01), if the \"address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing,\" the pharmacist is \"not required to enter any of these items on the prescription\" if the information is \"otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled,\" or if \"The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. Additionally, if the \"prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist,\" the pharmacist is \"responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources imply a responsibility for pharmacists to exercise professional judgment or make good faith efforts when information is not immediately complete or available. The policy document mentions \"professional judgement in filling prescriptions\" for emergency situations [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01], and the provider manual mentions being \"otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate\" and making a \"good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual provides specific guidance for missing patient demographic information (address, age, CIN) and unknown prescriber identity, outlining conditions under which entry is not required and when verification efforts are needed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].", "The policy documents, specifically the newest relevant one, focus on the scenario of an unavailable PCP for emergency department prescriptions, requiring professional judgment to fill and subsequent contact with the PCP [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01].", "The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacists are *not required to enter* certain missing patient details under specific conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy document focuses on actions to take (fill, contact PCP) rather than data entry requirements."], "caveats": null}
